full_response:
We start from the Schwarzschild line-element  

ds² = −(1 − 2GM/r) dt² + dr² /(1 − 2GM/r) + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²) .       (1)

We are instructed to imagine continuing the coordinate r through the curvature singularity at r = 0 into negative values, r < 0.  Although no physical observer can get there (the curvature K = R_{abcd}R^{abcd} ∝ 1/r⁶ blows up at r = 0), nothing forbids us mathematically from asking what the metric coefficients look like for r < 0.

Define the usual Schwarzschild factor  

f(r) ≡ 1 − 2GM/r = (r − 2GM)/r .                              (2)

Sign of f(r) for r < 0  

• r < 0  ⇒  r − 2GM < 0 (because 2GM > 0)  
    and r < 0 ⇒ denominator < 0.

Hence  
f(r) = (negative)/(negative) > 0.                                    (3)

Indeed  

f(r) = 1 − 2GM/r = 1 + 2GM/|r| > 1 .                              (4)

Therefore  

g_tt = −f(r)  <  0 ,    |g_tt| > 1,                                       (5)  
g_rr = +1/f(r)  >  0 .                                                 (6)

Interpretation of signs  

• A negative coefficient in front of dt² with our (−+++ ) signature means t is the timelike coordinate.  
• A positive coefficient for dr² means r remains spacelike.

Thus, everywhere in the “fifth region” r < 0, the coordinate t is once again timelike, exactly as it is in the ordinary external Schwarzschild regions (I and III) with r > 2GM.  One can draw an additional disconnected region of the Kruskal diagram (sometimes called region V) in which t is timelike, but this region is separated from our universe by the true curvature singularity at r = 0, so no causal curve from our universe can enter it.

\boxed{\text{Yes. For all r<0 the Schwarzschild factor }f(r)>0,\;g_{tt}=-f(r)<0,\text{ so the coordinate }t\text{ is timelike there.}}  

Confidence: 80%
extracted_answer: \text{Yes. For all r<0 the Schwarzschild factor }f(r)>0,\;g_{tt}=-f(r)<0,\text{ so the coordinate }t\text{ is timelike there.}
